questions.text: The compressibility factor Z=PV/RT for 1 mol of a real gas is greater than unity at a pressure of 1 atm and 273.15 K. The molar volume of the gas at STP will be :
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: The gas is less compressible than ideal gas. Hence, Vm>22.4L

The compressibility factor Z of a gas is defined as the ratio of the product of pressure P and volume V to the product of the number of moles n, universal gas constant R, and temperature T. Mathematically, it is expressed as: Z=PV/nRT At STP (Standard Temperature and Pressure), which is defined as 0°C (273.15 K) and 1 atm, the molar volume of an ideal gas is 22.4 L. If the compressibility factor of a gas is greater than unity at a pressure of 1 atm and 273.15 K, then the gas behaves less ideally.In such cases, the molar volume of the gas at STP will be greater than 22.4 L for an ideal gas.So in short we can say,the gas is less compressible than ideal gas. Hence, Vm>22.4L.
The gas is less compressible than ideal gas. Hence, Vm>22.4L

Hydrogen bonding is a type of intermolecular force that occurs between a hydrogen atom that is bonded to a highly electronegative atom (such as oxygen or nitrogen) and an atom that has a lone pair of electrons (such as oxygen). In the case of ice, the hydrogen atoms in the water molecules are bonded to the oxygen atoms, which are highly electronegative. This means that the hydrogen atoms have a partial positive charge, and the oxygen atoms have a partial negative charge. options.explanation: Liquids exist over a narrow range of temperature and pressure because the intermolecular forces between the liquid particles are strong enough to hold them together, but not so strong that they prevent them from moving around. The strength of the intermolecular forces in a liquid depends on the type of intermolecular forces present. The most common types of intermolecular forces are dipole-dipole forces, London dispersion forces, and hydrogen bonding. The higher the temperature of a liquid, the faster the liquid particles move and the weaker the intermolecular forces become. When the temperature reaches the boiling point, the liquid vaporises. The higher the pressure of a liquid, the closer the liquid particles are forced together and the stronger the intermolecular forces become.

questions.text: SI unit of Boyle law is:
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: Miscellaneous

options.explanation: Boyle's law states that for a fixed mass of an ideal gas kept at a fixed temperature, pressure and volume are inversely proportional. This means that if pressure is increased, volume will decrease, and vice versa. The SI unit of pressure is Pascal (Pa), which is defined as Newton per square metre (N/m²). The SI unit of volume is cubic metre (m³). Therefore, the SI unit of Boyle's law is Nm, which is Newton-metre. P₁V₁ = P₂V₂ As you can see, the units of pressure and volume cancel out, leaving us with the unit of Newton-metre.

questions.text: According to Charles Law increase in volume corresponds to increased temperature on_ scale.
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: Miscellaneous

options.explanation: The Celsius scale is a relative scale of temperature. This means that zero on the Celsius scale does not correspond to a complete stop of molecular motion. Instead, zero on the Celsius scale corresponds to the freezing point of water. This can cause problems when using Charles' law with temperatures in Celsius, because the volume of a gas will not increase or decrease linearly with temperature.

The Kelvin scale is used in Charles' law because it is an absolute scale of temperature. This means that zero on the Kelvin scale corresponds to a complete stop of molecular motion. Charles' law states that the volume of a gas is directly proportional to its absolute temperature. This means that if the temperature of a gas increases, its volume will increase as well. Conversely, if the temperature of a gas decreases, its volume will decrease as well.

The Fahrenheit scale is not used in Charles's Law because it is not an absolute scale of temperature. This means that zero on the Fahrenheit scale does not correspond to a complete stop of molecular motion. Instead, zero on the Fahrenheit scale corresponds to the freezing point of brine. This can cause problems when using Charles's Law with temperatures in Fahrenheit, because the volume of a gas will not increase or decrease linearly with temperature.

options.explanation: Evaporation is a cooling process because of the kinetic energy transfer that occurs during the phase change of a substance. When a substance evaporates, the high-energy molecules at the surface have enough kinetic energy to overcome the intermolecular attractions and escape into the surrounding environment. As the higher energy molecules leave the substance, they take away some of the thermal energy (heat) with them, thereby decreasing the average kinetic energy and temperature of the remaining molecules in the substance. This decrease in temperature results in a cooling effect. Therefore, evaporation is a cooling process due to the transfer of thermal energy from higher energy molecules to the surrounding environment.

options.explanation: Electrostatic force is the force of attraction between oppositely charged particles. In ionic solids, the particles are ions, which are atoms or molecules that have lost or gained electrons. The ions are arranged in a lattice, with oppositely charged ions alternating. The strong electrostatic force between the ions in an ionic solid makes it difficult for the ions to escape from the lattice. This is why ionic solids have low volatility. Each face-centred atom is shared by 4 unit cells, so it only contributes 1/4 of an atom to each unit cell. Therefore, the total number of Na+ ions in an fcc unit cell is 8(1/8) + 6(1/4) = 4.

options.explanation: In the crystal structure of NaCl, the chloride ions (Cl-) are arranged in a face-centred cubic (fcc) lattice. This means that there is a chloride ion at each corner and at the centre of each face of the unit cell. The sodium ions (Na+) are arranged in a body-centred cubic (bcc) lattice, which means that there is a sodium ion at the centre of the unit cell and at each corner. The sodium and chloride ions are arranged in a checkerboard pattern, with the sodium ions occupying the tetrahedral voids and the chloride ions occupying the octahedral voids.

The structure of NaCl is not body centred cubic (bcc) because it is an ionic compound. In an ionic compound, the ions are arranged in a way that minimises the energy of the system. This is done by having the ions arranged in a lattice that allows them to be as close together as possible.

As you can see, a face-centred cubic lattice is the most efficient way to pack ions together. This is why the structure of NaCl is not hexagonal or polygonal. In addition, the crystal structure of NaCl is also determined by the size of the sodium and chloride ions. The sodium ions are smaller than the chloride ions, and they can fit into the tetrahedral and octahedral voids in the face-centred cubic lattice. A hexagonal or polygonal lattice would not be able to accommodate the different sizes of the sodium and chloride ions

options.explanation: The reason why most matter around us is in the form of a solid is because solids exhibit certain characteristics that distinguish them from liquids and gases. For example, solids have a fixed volume and a fixed shape and cannot flow because their particles cannot move from place to place. They also cannot be compressed because their particles are close together.
Although liquids are common on Earth, this state of matter is relatively rare in the universe because liquids only exist over a narrow temperature and pressure range.

questions.explanation: Liquids exist over a narrow range of temperature and pressure because the intermolecular forces between the liquid particles are strong enough to hold them together, but not so strong that they prevent them from moving around. The strength of the intermolecular forces in a liquid depends on the type of intermolecular forces present. The most common types of intermolecular forces are dipole-dipole forces, London dispersion forces, and hydrogen bonding. The higher the temperature of a liquid, the faster the liquid particles move and the weaker the intermolecular forces become. When the temperature reaches the boiling point, the liquid vaporises. The higher the pressure of a liquid, the closer the liquid particles are forced together and the stronger the intermolecular forces become.

options.explanation: Avagadros law states that equal volumes of all gases at the same temperature and pressure must contain equal number of molecules.

The Arrhenius equation helps explain how the rate of a chemical reaction changes with temperature. It shows that as temperature increases, the rate of the reaction also increases, and it quantifies the relationship between temperature, activation energy, and reaction rate.

Moseley's Law, established by physicist Henry Moseley in 1913, revolutionized the periodic table. It links the square root of the frequency of X-ray spectral lines emitted by elements to their atomic number (proton count), replacing the previous classification based on atomic weights. This law clarified the periodic table, aligning elements with their true properties and laying a solid foundation for modern atomic theory and understanding of the atom's structure. Moseley's contribution remains a cornerstone in atomic and nuclear physics, reshaping our comprehension of the elements.

The Joule-Thomson effect, also known as throttling, is a phenomenon in thermodynamics. It occurs when a gas undergoes a sudden expansion through a valve or small opening, which results in a decrease in temperature. This cooling effect is due to the conversion of some of the gas's internal energy into work done against the surrounding environment during the expansion. It's a fundamental principle often observed in various industrial and scientific applications, such as in refrigeration and liquefied natural gas production.

Boyle's law states that for a fixed mass of an ideal gas kept at a fixed temperature, pressure and volume are inversely proportional. This means that if pressure is increased, volume will decrease, and vice versa. The SI unit of pressure is Pascal (Pa), which is defined as Newton per square metre (N/m²). The SI unit of volume is cubic metre (m³). Therefore, the SI unit of Boyle's law is Nm, which is Newton-metre. P₁V₁ = P₂V₂ As you can see, the units of pressure and volume cancel out, leaving us with the unit of Newton-metre.

Intermolecular forces: The strength of the intermolecular forces in a liquid also affects vapour pressure. The three main types of intermolecular forces are dipole-dipole forces, hydrogen bonding, and London dispersion forces.

options.explanation: Evaporation is a cooling process because of the kinetic energy transfer that occurs during the phase change of a substance. When a substance evaporates, the high-energy molecules at the surface have enough kinetic energy to overcome the intermolecular attractions and escape into the surrounding environment. This decrease in temperature results in a cooling effect. Therefore, evaporation is a cooling process due to the transfer of thermal energy from higher energy molecules to the surrounding environment.

Polar molecules are molecules that have a net dipole moment. This means that the molecule has a positive end and a negative end. Polar molecules are attracted to each other by dipole-dipole forces. Not all molecular solids are polar. For example, nonpolar molecules, such as methane (CH4), do not have a net dipole moment. As a result, nonpolar molecules are held together by London dispersion forces, which are the weakest type of intermolecular force. London dispersion forces are only significant when the molecules are large and have polarizable electron clouds.Nonpolar molecular solids: methane (CH4), ethane (C2H6), carbon dioxide (CO2)

Molecular solids are solids that are composed of molecules held together by intermolecular forces. These forces are much weaker than the covalent or ionic bonds that hold atoms or ions together in other types of solids, such as ionic or covalent solids. As a result, molecular solids are typically soft and have low melting points.
